title: Phase 1 — Slice 1: Skeleton + Auralis on calibration tone (in-process)
description:
# Phase 1: Skeleton + Auralis on calibration tone

**Plan reference:** Phase 1, Slice 1.

## What slice 1 ships

A user can run `cargo run --bin clitunes` and immediately see a flagship-quality Auralis spectrum visualiser reacting to a built-in calibration tone (440 Hz sine with 0.5 Hz amplitude modulation). No audio output, no source picking, no daemon, no config — just the visualiser pipeline working end-to-end against a deterministic signal source. **The first PR a user looks at must already feel like a flagship product.** This is the smallest demo-able slice that justifies the visualiser-first identity.

## Why "no audio output"

Slice 1 deliberately does not output audio. The visualiser pipeline is the load-bearing claim — proving it works on a deterministic signal removes audio sources, decoders, ring buffers, and OS audio APIs as confounding variables. Slice 2 adds the radio source. Slice 3 adds the daemon split.

## Architecture posture

Slice 1 uses **throwaway in-process scaffolding** for the visualiser pipeline (per D12). The visualiser code, the wgpu pipeline, the Kitty writer, the calibration tone source, and the FFT tap all live inside the `clitunes` binary process. No daemon. No control socket. No SPMC ring (in-process bounded channel instead). The throwaway parts are exactly the parts that the daemon split (slice 3) replaces, and they are kept intentionally narrow so the retrofit cost is bounded.

## Units in this phase

- **Unit 2** — Workspace + 3-crate scaffolding + CI baseline + cargo-deny.
- **Unit 3** — Calibration tone source + realfft tap + bounded SPSC ring (in-process).
- **Unit 4** — Visualiser trait + wgpu pipeline + Kitty graphics writer + Auralis bare-bones spectrum + integration in `clitunes` main.

## Success gate

`cargo run --bin clitunes` shows Auralis reacting to the calibration tone, hitting the Phase 0 committed frame budget on the developer's machine. `q` quits cleanly. No crashes. No frame drops over 60 seconds.
close_reason: Phase 1 epic complete. Slice 1 shipped: clitunes binary runs calibration tone → PcmRing → FftTap → Auralis → Kitty graphics protocol on Metal at ~55fps. E2E harness validates with 7/7 assertions. All child beads closed (Units 1,2,3,4,xb2). D15 invariant (clitunesd depgraph has zero wgpu/ratatui/crossterm) verified. CI+cargo-deny+e2e workflows added.

title: Unit 13: Client refactor — replace in-process scaffolding with control-bus + mmap visualiser tap
description:
# Unit 13: Client refactor (in-process → daemon split)

**Phase:** 3 (Slice 3). **Parent epic:** clitunes-d50.

## Goal

Gut the in-process scaffolding from slices 1–2. Rebuild the `clitunes` client around the control-bus client (Unit 10) and the cross-process visualiser tap (Unit 11). After this unit, the client and daemon are real separate processes; everything that worked in slice 2 still works, just over the daemon split. Two clients can attach to the same daemon, kill-and-respawn the daemon, all the load-bearing daemon-architecture promises hold.

## Requirements

R14 (daemon-client architecture is real), R15 (clients control daemon over the protocol), R17 (daemon survives client crashes), R18 (auto-spawn from client).

## Files

- `crates/clitunes/src/main.rs` — REWRITTEN. Removes the in-process calibration source / FFT tap / wgpu pipeline / Auralis wiring. Replaces with: auto-spawn (Unit 9), control-bus connect (Unit 10), cross-process PCM tap subscribe (Unit 11), a render loop that pulls from the tap and feeds Auralis (Unit 4 — same trait, same code, different data source).
- `crates/clitunes/src/client/render_loop.rs` — the client's main render loop.
- `crates/clitunes/src/client/control_session.rs` — wraps the control-bus client with a higher-level API the client UI uses (`session.play()`, `session.pause()`, `session.subscribe_now_playing()`, etc.).
- `crates/clitunes/src/client/reconnect.rs` — when the control socket EOFs (daemon crashed/restarted), the client triggers auto-spawn and reattaches transparently.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/daemon/event_loop.rs` — daemon-side: the main event loop that wires together the control bus server (Unit 10), the PCM ring producer side (Unit 11), the source pipeline (Units 5–7 + 14), the player state machine.
- `crates/clitunes/src/bin/clitunesd.rs` — REWRITTEN to actually be the daemon (was placeholder in Unit 2). Sets up tracing, runs the daemon event loop.
- `docs/architecture.md` — daemon-client architecture doc with the ASCII diagram from the plan, expanded.
- `crates/clitunes/tests/client_reconnect_tests.rs`
- `crates/clitunes-engine/tests/daemon_event_loop_tests.rs`

## Approach

1. **Daemon event loop:** spawn the control bus server on one tokio task, the source pipeline on another, the PCM ring producer on another. The source pipeline writes into the PCM ring (Unit 11 producer side), the control bus server broadcasts state changes to subscribed clients, and the picker/source-switching verbs are dispatched from the control bus through an internal mpsc to the source pipeline.
2. **Client connection sequence:**
   - Auto-spawn (Unit 9) — connect to control socket, retry up to 2s, spawn daemon if needed.
   - Banner exchange (Unit 10) — verify protocol version.
   - Subscribe to events: `now_playing`, `state`, `pcm_meta`, `errors`.
   - Subscribe to PCM tap: `pcm_subscribe` verb returns the path to the consumer's mmap region (Path 1) or sets up the datagram socket (Path 2). The client maps the region.
   - Read state.toml — if `picker_seen == false`, send `picker` verb to ask the daemon to put the picker in front of the visualiser pane.
   - Start render loop.
3. **Render loop:** at the committed framerate (60 or 30 fps), pull recent PCM frames from the cross-process tap, feed to Auralis (`on_pcm` + `on_fft`), call `render_gpu` (or `render_tui` based on visualiser), encode via the Kitty writer, write to stdout. Listen for keyboard events on a separate tokio task; on `q`, send `quit` to the daemon and exit.
4. **Reconnect:** if the control socket reads EOF unexpectedly (daemon crashed or restarted), the client:
   - Logs the disconnect.
   - Sleeps 200ms (backoff).
   - Re-runs the connection sequence (auto-spawn + banner + subscribe + tap).
   - Resumes the render loop where it left off.
   - During the disconnect window, the visualiser keeps rendering whatever frame data is in the local buffer (Auralis fades to silence smoothly rather than freezing).
5. **Throwaway scope cleanup:** delete the in-process scaffolding files from Phase 1 (the in-process FFT tap stays as a producer-side accumulator inside the daemon, but the in-process ring API is replaced by the cross-process tap).

## Test scenarios

- Happy: `cargo run --bin clitunes` auto-spawns the daemon, connects, plays, renders Auralis. Visually identical to slice 2's behavior.
- Happy: two `clitunes` clients running simultaneously. Both render Auralis from the same audio source. Both receive `now_playing_changed` events.
- Happy: kill the daemon. Both clients detect EOF, reconnect (one of them auto-spawns a new daemon, the other reattaches), playback resumes.
- Happy: kill one client. The daemon's idle timer does NOT start (other client still attached). The remaining client is unaffected.
- Edge: control bus banner mismatch. Client logs error and exits with a clear message.
- Edge: daemon's PCM ring is overrun. Client receives `Overrun` from the tap, skips one frame, continues.
- Edge: daemon takes >2s to start during auto-spawn. Client fails with "daemon failed to start within 2 seconds; check $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR permissions and ~/.cache/clitunes/clitunesd.log."
- Edge: terminal does not support Kitty graphics. Client surfaces the same error path as Unit 4 (with the Cascade hint, since by Unit 18 Cascade exists).
- Integration: SC2 dry-run. 30-day soak (manual, not in CI) — the daemon and client run continuously, surviving sleep/wake on macOS, network drops, station changes.

## Verification

- Tests pass.
- D15 enforcement check from Unit 2 STILL passes (the daemon binary's deps are unchanged; this unit only adds daemon-side glue + client-side glue).
- `docs/architecture.md` is committed and matches the implementation.
- Manual: kill the daemon → both clients reconnect within 2 seconds → playback resumes from the same source.

## Slice 3 success criterion

Two clients attached to one daemon, daemon survives killing one client, daemon respawns when killed and the other client reconnects. **This is the slice 3 demo and proves the daemon split is real.**

title: Unit 16: Standalone --pane clients + headless verbs + clitunes status --json
description:
# Unit 16: Standalone --pane clients + headless verbs

**Phase:** 4 (Slice 4). **Parent epic:** clitunes-eme.

## Goal

Wire `clitunes --pane <name>` as a standalone-process client that subscribes to the daemon and renders one component fullscreen. Implement the headless control verbs (`play`, `pause`, etc.) and `clitunes status --json` as one-shot clients. **This is what makes clitunes the "Ghostty of TUI music apps" — it doesn't just live in its own window; it embeds in tmux status lines, side panes, status-line scripts, and system media keys.**

## Requirements

R18, R19, R20.

## Files

- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/cli/pane_mode.rs` — `--pane` mode dispatcher.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/cli/headless_verbs.rs` — `play`/`pause`/`next`/etc. one-shot dispatchers.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/cli/status_command.rs` — `clitunes status --json` implementation.
- `crates/clitunes/src/main.rs` — modified to dispatch on argv shape: `clitunes [paths]` (full UI), `clitunes --pane <name> [--viz <viz>]` (single pane), `clitunes <verb>` (headless control), `clitunes status --json` (one-shot status).
- `crates/clitunes/tests/cli_pane_mode_tests.rs`
- `crates/clitunes/tests/cli_headless_verbs_tests.rs`

## Approach

### `--pane <name>` (subscriber clients)

Each `--pane` invocation is a standalone process that connects to the daemon, subscribes to the relevant events + (if needed) the PCM tap, and renders ONE component fullscreen with no chrome.

- `clitunes --pane visualiser [--viz auralis|tideline|cascade]` — fullscreen visualiser only. Default `--viz auralis`. Auralis and Tideline use the wgpu+Kitty pipeline; Cascade uses the unicode-block path (no Kitty deps required, runs over SSH).
- `clitunes --pane now-playing` — text-only now-playing strip with optional embedded album art (Kitty graphics) at the left.
- `clitunes --pane mini-spectrum` — small spectrum bars in pure unicode block characters (no wgpu, no Kitty graphics), intended for status-line embedding in a 1-row-tall slot. Effectively a tiny preset of Cascade's CPU renderer.
- All `--pane` clients respect `q` to quit and clean up the daemon connection.

### Headless verbs

`clitunes play|pause|next|prev|volume|source|vis` — connects, sends the corresponding command via the control bus, exits 0 on response. Suitable for system media key bindings (the user binds Cmd+P → `clitunes play` in their WM/launcher).

### `clitunes status --json`

Connects, sends `status` verb, prints pretty-printed JSON to stdout, exits 0. Suitable for status-line shell integration (e.g., a 5-second tmux status-line refresh loop).

### Argument dispatch

Use `clap` derive macros. Top-level subcommand match:
- No subcommand + paths → full UI (Unit 13).
- `--pane <name>` flag → pane mode.
- `play|pause|next|prev|volume|source|vis|status` subcommand → headless dispatch.

## Test scenarios

- Happy: `clitunes --pane mini-spectrum` runs in a 1-row slot inside tmux, displays bars, exits on `q`.
- Happy: `clitunes --pane visualiser` runs fullscreen, displays Auralis identical to the default UI's visualiser pane.
- Happy: `clitunes --pane visualiser --viz cascade` runs fullscreen Cascade, demonstrating CPU-only rendering on terminals without Kitty graphics.
- Happy: `clitunes status --json` prints `{"track":..., "source":..., "position":..., "duration":..., "visualiser":..., "layout":...}` and exits 0.
- Happy: `clitunes play` connects, plays, exits 0. `clitunes pause` connects, pauses, exits 0.
- Edge: `clitunes --pane mini-spectrum` in a single-column-tall pane with no bottom border. Renders correctly.
- Edge: `clitunes status --json` when daemon not running. Auto-spawns daemon (Unit 9), prints status, daemon idles after exit.
- Edge: two `--pane visualiser` clients running simultaneously. Both render the same audio. Validates the multi-client SPMC ring (Unit 11) end-to-end.
- Failure: `clitunes --pane fnord` (unknown pane). Exits with "unknown pane: fnord. Available: visualiser, now-playing, mini-spectrum."
- Integration: real tmux session with a `mini-spectrum` in the status bar, a `visualiser` pane in a side window, and a coding editor in the main pane, all running for 30 minutes without crashes (manual SC3 dry-run).

## Verification

- Tests pass.
- Manual: real-world tmux/wezterm/ghostty embedding scenarios match SC3.
- `clitunes status --json` integrated into a sample status-line shell snippet that updates every 5 seconds without leaking processes.

## Pane content sketches reference

- `visualiser` pane: see Unit 4. Min 20×5.
- `now-playing` pane: 1 row tall minimum (just `Artist - Title`); 3 rows when there's room (artist, title, album). Optional 5-cell-wide embedded album art on the left at full height. Min 20×1.
- `mini-spectrum` pane: 1 row tall minimum, fills width with unicode block chars (`▁▂▃▄▅▆▇█`) showing 16-bin log spectrum from the FFT tap. No labels, no axes. Min 8×1.

title: Unit 8: Curated 12-station picker overlay + state.toml persistence + engineer-taste audit
description:
# Unit 8: Curated picker + state.toml persistence

**Phase:** 2 (Slice 2). **Parent epic:** clitunes-70w.

## Goal

Build the curated 12-station picker overlay that's the answer to the "no taste imposition" feedback memory. On first launch, the picker overlays Auralis (which is rendering the calibration tone behind it), the user picks a station, the choice is persisted to `state.toml` with mode 0600, and the next launch goes straight to playback.

**The picker IS the zero-config experience.** No hardcoded default station — every taste-laden choice is offered as one slot in a curated picker spanning genres, moods, and regions, and the user makes the choice once.

## Requirements

R1 (zero-config first run), R7 (curated stations selectable from a picker), R26 (first-pixel ≤3s), SC1, SC2.

## Files

- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/tui/picker/mod.rs` — picker overlay component (ratatui widget rendered on top of the visualiser pane).
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/tui/picker/curated_seed.rs` — the 12 curated stations as a static `&[CuratedStation]`. **Audited per memory feedback** to span ≥6 genres, ≥3 regions, ≥3 moods, with no two slots being slight variations of the same station. Documented in `docs/curation/2026-04-11-curated-stations.md` with rationale per slot.
- `crates/clitunes-core/src/state.rs` — `State { last_station_uuid, last_visualiser, last_layout, picker_seen: bool }`. Serde TOML serialise/deserialise.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/tui/picker/state_persist.rs` — load/save `state.toml`. **Set umask(0o177) before creating the file so it's mode 0600 atomically.** Parent dir created with mode 0700. Per round-2 SEC-011.
- `docs/curation/2026-04-11-curated-stations.md` — the curated list, rationale per slot, and the explicit "engineer taste audit" rubric the seed must pass.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/tests/tui_picker_tests.rs`
- `crates/clitunes-core/tests/state_persist_tests.rs`

## The 12-slot curated seed

Indicative slots (final list lives in `curated_seed.rs` after the audit; this is the audit rubric):

1. UK indie/alt — BBC Radio 6 Music
2. Public radio talk + music mix — KEXP Seattle
3. European electronic — SomaFM Groove Salad (or similar)
4. Latin — a major Spanish-language station (e.g., Radio Continental Buenos Aires or Radio Activa)
5. African — a strong African continental station (e.g., Capital FM Kenya or Radio Africa)
6. Jazz — WBGO Newark
7. Classical — France Musique
8. Hip-hop — a major hip-hop station (e.g., Hot 97 NYC or BBC 1Xtra)
9. Asian — a Japanese or Korean station (e.g., FM Yokohama or J-Wave)
10. Ambient/chill — SomaFM Drone Zone or Lush
11. World/global — Radio FIP (France) or Radio Nacional Argentina
12. Live news — BBC World Service

**Audit rubric:** before merging, verify the list spans:
- ≥6 distinct genre buckets
- ≥3 continents
- ≥3 distinct mood/energy ranges (high-energy / mid / chill)
- No two slots that are minor variations of the same station
- Each slot has a written rationale in the curation doc

The audit is enforced by the curation doc's explicit checklist; reviewers must check off each item before approving the seed.

## Approach

1. **Picker UI:** centred 60×24 popup over the visualiser pane. Ratatui `Block` with rounded borders, title "Pick a starting point". 12 rows; each row is `<num>. <name> — <genre> — <country>` colored by genre family. Arrow keys / `j`/`k` navigate; Enter selects; `q` quits without selecting (in which case the calibration tone keeps playing).
2. **First-launch detection:** on startup, load `state.toml`. If `picker_seen == false`, show the picker overlay. On selection, set `picker_seen = true`, write the chosen `last_station_uuid`, save the file. Subsequent launches skip the picker and go straight to the last station.
3. **State.toml persistence (PER ROUND-2 SEC-011):**
   - Parent dir: `~/.config/clitunes/`. Create with mode 0700 if it doesn't exist.
   - File: `~/.config/clitunes/state.toml`. **Set umask(0o177) immediately before opening the file with O_CREAT** so the file is created mode 0600 atomically (no race window where the file is briefly readable by other users).
   - Atomic write: write to `state.toml.tmp`, fsync, rename to `state.toml`. This prevents a torn write if the process crashes mid-save.
4. **Picker is rendered ON TOP of Auralis** — Auralis keeps running the calibration tone behind the picker, the picker is a translucent overlay (or, more practically, a solid centred box with a slightly dimmed background). The visual effect is "the visualiser is alive even before you've picked a station," reinforcing the visualiser-first identity from the very first frame.
5. **Calibration tone is visually distinguishable from real music** — Unit 19 polishes this further, but the seed of the work lands here: tune the calibration tone amplitude envelope so the picker context feels visually quieter than typical music, signalling "this is a placeholder."
6. **Search box:** typing `/` in the picker filters the 12 stations by name match. The filter is debounced 100ms. Stations not matching fade out.

## Test scenarios

- Happy: first launch. `state.toml` doesn't exist. Picker shows. User selects station 3. State file is created with mode 0600. Next launch skips picker.
- Happy: subsequent launch with `picker_seen = true`. Picker doesn't show; picker is reachable via `:picker` command (added to Unit 10's verb list).
- Edge: `state.toml` exists but is malformed (truncated or hand-edited badly). Code logs the error, falls back to picker, doesn't crash.
- Edge: parent dir `~/.config/clitunes/` doesn't exist. Code creates it with mode 0700.
- Edge: parent dir exists but is mode 0755. Code logs a warning ("config dir is world-readable; consider chmod 700") but continues.
- Edge: terminal too small to render the 60×24 popup (e.g., 50×20). Picker falls back to a minimal list view (no styling, just numbered rows).
- Security: after first save, `stat ~/.config/clitunes/state.toml` reports mode 0600. Verified in the test.
- Security: deliberately create the file mode 0644 first; the code overwrites with 0600 on next save.
- Curation audit: a unit test reads `curated_seed.rs` and asserts ≥6 distinct genres, ≥3 distinct countries, 12 entries total. (The semantic audit lives in the curation doc; this is the mechanical floor.)

## Verification

- Tests pass.
- Curation doc exists at `docs/curation/2026-04-11-curated-stations.md` with the audit checklist filled in.
- Manual: run `clitunes` with no `state.toml`. Picker overlays Auralis. Pick station 3. Re-run. Picker doesn't show. Last selection plays.
- `stat ~/.config/clitunes/state.toml` shows mode 0600.

## Memory pin

This unit is the implementation of `feedback_no_taste_imposition.md`. Any future work that adds a "default station" or "default visualiser" or "default theme" must follow the same pattern: present a curated picker, never hardcode taste.

title: Unit 14: Local files source (CLI args, folder scan, lofty tags, queue)
description:
# Unit 14: Local files source

**Phase:** 4 (Slice 4). **Parent epic:** clitunes-eme.

## Goal

Implement the local files source: `clitunes ~/Music/album.flac` plays a single file; `clitunes ~/Music` recursively scans the folder and queues all supported files in directory order. Tag reads via `lofty`. **No library, no SQLite, no tag database** — clitunes is a player, not a music library manager.

## Requirements

R4 (local file playback), R5 (sources), R8 (source switching), R20 (next/prev).

## Files

- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/sources/local/mod.rs` — `LocalSource` struct implementing the `Source` trait.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/sources/local/folder_scan.rs` — `walkdir`-based recursive scan with extension filter and `local.scan.max_files` cap.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/sources/local/queue.rs` — `VecDeque<Track>` queue with `next`/`prev` semantics.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/sources/local/symphonia_file.rs` — symphonia `MediaSource` for local files (`File`-backed, `is_seekable() = true`).
- `crates/clitunes-core/src/track.rs` — `Track { path, title, artist, album, albumartist, track_num, year, duration, embedded_art }`. All free-text fields **sanitised at construction via `clitunes_core::untrusted_string::sanitize`** (per round-2 SEC-004 + D20 — MP3/FLAC/Vorbis tags are arbitrary user data; a doctored tag could embed terminal control sequences).
- `crates/clitunes/src/bin/clitunesd.rs` — register `LocalSource` in the source registry.
- `crates/clitunes/src/main.rs` — CLI args parsing for paths.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/tests/sources_local_source_tests.rs`.

## Approach

1. **CLI parsing:** `clitunes [PATH...]` (each path is a file or directory; optional). Files are added to the queue in argv order. Directories are recursively walked via `walkdir`, files are filtered by extension (`.mp3 .flac .ogg .opus .wav .m4a .aac`), sorted alphabetically by full path, then added.
2. **Queue:** `VecDeque<Track>`. `next` pops front and starts playing it; `prev` pushes the current track back to the front and pops the previous from a separate `played: VecDeque<Track>` history. Capacity is unbounded (typical music library is small enough that holding 10k tracks in memory is fine).
3. **Tag extraction:** `lofty::Probe::open(path)?.read()?` returns a `TaggedFile`. Extract title, artist, album, albumartist, track number, year, duration, and the first picture's bytes (for embedded album art). Sanitise every string field.
4. **`LocalSource::next_packet`:** opens the current file via symphonia (with `is_seekable() = true`, since this is a local file with random-access support). Decodes packets, pushes to the PCM ring (Unit 11 producer side). On EOF, advances to the next track in the queue.
5. **Source switching:** the `:source local <path>` and `:source radio <uuid>` control verbs (Unit 10) tear down the current source and bring up the new one. The PCM ring keeps flowing — old data drains out, new data flows in. Visualiser is unaware of the swap (it just sees a brief silence).
6. **Embedded album art:** lofty's `Picture::data() -> &[u8]` gives the JPEG/PNG bytes. Cached per-track in memory. Passed to the now-playing pane (Unit 13's pane component) for Kitty graphics image display in slice 4 polish — but the actual image transmission is in slice 4 polish, not Unit 14. Unit 14 only stores the bytes.
7. **DoS protection:** `local.scan.max_files` (default 50_000). If a directory exceeds the limit, the scan stops, queues the first N, surfaces a warning. Prevents `clitunes /` from OOMing.

## Test scenarios

- Happy: `clitunes ~/Music/test.flac` plays the file, displays its tags in now-playing, exits cleanly when done.
- Happy: `clitunes ~/Music` scans, queues, plays in order, advances on track end.
- Happy: `next` and `prev` skip tracks within the queue.
- Happy: switching from radio to local mid-session (`:source local ~/Music/foo.flac`) tears down radio, brings up local, no frame drop on the visualiser.
- Edge: folder with 0 supported files. Source emits "no playable files in <path>".
- Edge: file with no tags. now-playing falls back to filename.
- Edge: file with embedded art >5MB. Art is loaded but the now-playing pane scales display via Kitty's c=/r= cell sizing.
- Edge: file is a symlink. Followed normally.
- Edge: file is corrupt. Decode error logged, source skips to next track.
- Edge: folder with 100_000 files. Scan capped at 50_000, warning surfaces.
- Edge: tag containing an ANSI escape sequence. Sanitiser strips it; stored Track has a clean string.
- Integration: `clitunes test.flac` with daemon already running attaches as a client, plays, visualiser is reactive.

## Verification

- Tests pass.
- Manual: scan a real ~/Music directory, play through 10 tracks, verify tag display matches reality.

title: Unit 7: Symphonia decoder pipeline (MP3/OGG/AAC) feeding the PCM ring
description:
# Unit 7: Symphonia decoder pipeline + cpal output

**Phase:** 2 (Slice 2). **Parent epic:** clitunes-70w.

## Goal

Decode the audio bytes coming out of Unit 5's radio stream (with Unit 6's ICY metadata stripped out) using `symphonia`, push the resulting PCM frames into the ring (Unit 3's API), and **also open a cpal output stream** that consumes from the same ring and feeds the OS audio device. After this unit, slice 2 has actual audio output for the first time.

## Requirements

R5 (radio playback), R4 (audio output works on Linux + macOS).

## Files

- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/sources/radio/decoder.rs` — symphonia integration: takes a `Box<dyn MediaSource>` (the byte stream from Unit 5 wrapped in a custom `MediaSource` that strips ICY metadata via Unit 6), runs the symphonia probe + decoder, pushes decoded PCM frames into the ring.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/pcm/cpal_output.rs` — cpal output stream: opens the default output device, picks a format compatible with 48kHz stereo f32, sets up an output callback that pulls from the ring and writes to the cpal buffer. Configurable via `audio.output_device` for users with multiple sound cards.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/sources/radio/icy_media_source.rs` — `MediaSource` impl that wraps the radio byte stream, strips ICY metadata blocks (publishing parsed events to the now-playing bus from Unit 6), and exposes only the audio bytes to symphonia.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/tests/sources_radio_decoder_tests.rs`
- `crates/clitunes-engine/tests/pcm_cpal_output_tests.rs`

## Approach

1. **MediaSource wrapping:** symphonia consumes a `MediaSource` (an async-or-sync byte stream with optional seek). For radio, the stream is non-seekable (`is_seekable() = false`). The wrapper reads the underlying reqwest byte stream, strips the ICY interleaved metadata blocks (via Unit 6's parser), and yields only audio bytes to symphonia.
2. **Codec probe:** `symphonia::default::get_probe().format(...)`, then iterate decoded packets via `Format::next_packet()`. v1 codec coverage: MP3 (most common), OGG/Vorbis (second most common), AAC (best-effort — symphonia's AAC is less battle-tested per the risk table).
3. **Format conversion:** symphonia decodes to whatever format the source uses (e.g., 44.1kHz interleaved s16). Convert to 48kHz stereo f32 via a simple resampler (`rubato::FftFixedIn` or symphonia's built-in resampler if available). The PCM ring is canonicalised on 48kHz stereo f32 — every source converts to that format before pushing.
4. **Ring push:** the decoder's output frames are pushed into the ring via `RingProducer::push_frames`. If the ring is full, the producer waits (the cpal callback drains it at real-time rate, so backpressure is natural).
5. **cpal output:** open the default device, query supported formats, prefer 48kHz stereo f32. If the device doesn't support that, fall back to whatever format it supports and resample on the output side. The output callback runs on cpal's high-priority audio thread; it reads from the ring with `RingConsumer::read_frames` and writes to the cpal buffer. Underruns (cpal callback runs when ring is empty) increment an `underruns` counter and are logged at trace level.
6. **Configurable output device:** `audio.output_device = "default"` (config key). Users with multiple sound cards can pick by name. cpal exposes device enumeration via `Host::output_devices()`.

## Test scenarios

- Happy: a sample MP3 file (loaded as a `MediaSource`) is decoded and pushed to the ring at the expected sample rate.
- Happy: a sample OGG/Vorbis file is decoded and pushed to the ring.
- Happy: a sample AAC file is decoded — best-effort, may be skipped if symphonia AAC is too unreliable for the test fixtures.
- Happy: cpal output stream opens on Linux and macOS, plays the decoded ring contents through the speakers.
- Happy: end-to-end: Unit 5's reqwest stream → Unit 6's ICY parser → Unit 7's decoder → ring → cpal output. Audio plays for 30 seconds without underruns.
- Edge: MP3 with malformed frame headers. symphonia reports the error; decoder logs and skips the bad packet; audio continues.
- Edge: OGG with embedded album art (Vorbis comment with cover.jpg). Decoder ignores the picture metadata; only audio frames go to the ring.
- Edge: cpal default device doesn't support 48kHz stereo f32. Fallback resampler kicks in. Audio still plays correctly.
- Edge: cpal underrun (ring drained faster than the decoder fills). Underrun counter increments; output briefly outputs silence; recovers when ring refills.
- Edge: sample rate mismatch — source is 44.1kHz, ring is 48kHz. Resampler bridges; visualiser FFT bins are still aligned to 48kHz.

## Verification

- Tests pass.
- Manual: a real radio stream from Unit 5 plays through speakers via Unit 7. Visualiser (Unit 4's Auralis) is now reacting to real music, not the calibration tone.

title: Unit 9: clitunesd binary lifecycle (fork+detach, flock singleton, idle-exit, auto-spawn)
description:
# Unit 9: clitunesd lifecycle + auto-spawn

**Phase:** 3 (Slice 3). **Parent epic:** clitunes-d50.

## Goal

Build the daemon binary's lifecycle: argv parsing, fork-and-detach, flock-based singleton enforcement, idle-exit timer (30 seconds after last client disconnect), log file rotation, and the client-side auto-spawn helper that launches `clitunesd` if no daemon is running. After this unit, `clitunes` and `clitunesd` are real separate processes; the daemon survives across multiple client connections; only one daemon can run per user.

## Requirements

R14 (daemon-client architecture), R17 (daemon idle-exits), R18 (auto-spawn).

## Files

- `crates/clitunes/src/bin/clitunesd.rs` — daemon main: argv parsing, daemon-mode setup, runs the daemon event loop.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/daemon/lifecycle.rs` — fork-and-detach (Linux/macOS via `nix::unistd::fork`), setsid, redirect stdio, write pidfile.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/daemon/singleton.rs` — flock-based singleton enforcement: open `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/clitunes/clitunesd.lock`, `flock(LOCK_EX | LOCK_NB)`. If the flock fails, another daemon is running — exit 0 silently. The lock is released on process death automatically (kernel-managed).
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/daemon/idle_timer.rs` — tracks the connected-client count. When count drops to 0, starts a 30-second timer. If a new client connects before the timer fires, cancel. If the timer fires, daemon exits cleanly (graceful shutdown — close PCM ring, close control socket, fsync state).
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/daemon/log_rotate.rs` — log file at `~/.cache/clitunes/clitunesd.log`, rotated at 10 MB to `clitunesd.log.1` (max 5 backups). Tracing subscriber writes there + stderr if foreground.
- `crates/clitunes/src/auto_spawn.rs` — client-side helper: try to connect to the control socket. If the socket doesn't exist or `connect` returns ECONNREFUSED, locate the `clitunesd` binary (resolve via `std::env::current_exe()` parent dir, then `$PATH`), spawn it with `Command::new(...).stdin(Stdio::null()).stdout(Stdio::null()).stderr(Stdio::null()).spawn()`, wait up to 2 seconds for the socket to appear, retry connect.
- `crates/clitunes/tests/auto_spawn_tests.rs`
- `crates/clitunes-engine/tests/daemon_singleton_tests.rs`

## Approach

1. **Daemon mode (default for clitunesd):** the daemon forks twice (the classic UNIX double-fork), calls `setsid` to detach from the controlling terminal, redirects stdin/stdout/stderr to `/dev/null`, writes a pidfile (informational only — singleton enforcement is via flock, not pidfile-based, so we're immune to stale-pidfile bugs). `--foreground` flag skips the fork and runs in the foreground for debugging.
2. **Singleton enforcement via flock:** open `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/clitunes/clitunesd.lock` (`$TMPDIR` on macOS), `flock(LOCK_EX | LOCK_NB)`. **Per the risk table:** flock is released on process death (Linux/macOS), so a crashed previous daemon can't leave a stale lock. This is why we use flock instead of a pidfile-based singleton.
3. **Idle-exit timer:** the daemon's main event loop tracks the connected-client count via the control socket's accept/disconnect events. When count → 0, start a `tokio::time::sleep(30s)`. If a new client connects, drop the sleep handle and reset. If the sleep completes, run graceful shutdown.
4. **Graceful shutdown:** close the control socket (refuse new connections), broadcast `daemon_shutting_down` event to any in-flight clients, drain in-flight messages, fsync `state.toml`, release the flock, exit 0.
5. **Auto-spawn:** the client tries to connect to `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/clitunes/clitunesd.sock`. On ECONNREFUSED or ENOENT, locate clitunesd:
   - First try: same dir as the client binary (`std::env::current_exe()?.parent()?.join("clitunesd")`). This is the cargo-installed and homebrew-installed layout.
   - Second try: `$PATH` lookup.
   - Failure: surface "could not find clitunesd; check installation."
6. **Spawning:** `Command::new(clitunesd_path).stdin(Stdio::null()).stdout(Stdio::null()).stderr(Stdio::null()).spawn()` returns immediately (the daemon backgrounds itself). The client then polls the socket every 100ms for up to 2 seconds.
7. **TOCTOU race fix (PER ROUND-2 SEC-001):** the control socket is created in Unit 12, but the umask trick is used here too — `umask(0o177)` is set immediately before any socket bind operation, so the socket inode is mode 0600 atomically (no race window between bind and chmod).

## Test scenarios

- Happy: `clitunesd` starts, forks, detaches, control socket appears at the expected path, daemon exits 30 seconds after no client connects.
- Happy: `clitunes` invokes auto_spawn, daemon starts, client connects within 2 seconds.
- Happy: two clitunesd processes started simultaneously. The second one's flock fails; it exits 0 silently. Only one daemon is running.
- Happy: client disconnects → idle timer starts → another client connects within 30s → idle timer cancelled → daemon stays alive.
- Edge: clitunesd binary not in PATH. auto_spawn fails with a clear error.
- Edge: `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` not set. Code falls back to `$TMPDIR/$USER/clitunes/`.
- Edge: log file at 10 MB. Rotation kicks in; `clitunesd.log.1` is created; main log truncated.
- Edge: SIGTERM sent to daemon. Daemon catches SIGTERM, runs graceful shutdown, exits 0.
- Edge: daemon crash (panic). flock is released by kernel; next clitunesd start succeeds.
- Edge: client connects, immediately disconnects. Daemon counts the disconnect, starts idle timer.
- Failure: flock acquisition fails for an unexpected reason (e.g., $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR unwritable). Daemon exits with a clear error.

## Verification

- Tests pass.
- Manual: start `clitunesd --foreground`, watch the logs as a client attaches and detaches; verify idle timer behavior.
- Manual: `clitunes` without a running daemon → daemon auto-spawns → playback works.

## What this unit does NOT include

- The control socket protocol (Unit 10).
- Socket security (Unit 12).
- The PCM ring (Unit 11).
- Client-side render loop (Unit 13).

title: Unit 20: Distribution — single static binary CI matrix + Homebrew + cargo install + AUR + tag v1.0.0
description:
# Unit 20: Distribution + v1.0.0 release

**Phase:** 5 (Slice 5). **Parent epic:** clitunes-z6r.

## Goal

Ship clitunes as a single static binary per platform with installation via Homebrew, `cargo install`, and AUR. Tag the v1.0.0 release.

## Requirements

R27.

## Files

- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` — modified to add release matrix on tag.
- `.github/workflows/release.yml` — release-on-tag workflow.
- `dist/homebrew/clitunes.rb` — Homebrew formula template.
- `dist/aur/PKGBUILD` — AUR PKGBUILD.
- `README.md` — replaces the Unit 2 placeholder. Content per the brainstorm's distribution section, including the 3-visualiser identity.
- `CHANGELOG.md` — starting with v1.0.0.
- `LICENSE` — final pick (MIT or Apache-2.0; default to MIT unless user overrides — supersedes Unit 2's placeholder pick).

## Approach

1. **Release CI on tags `v*`:** build for `aarch64-apple-darwin`, `x86_64-apple-darwin`, `x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu`, `aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu`. Strip binaries. Tar+gzip with checksums. Upload as GitHub release artifacts.
2. **Static linking:** use `cargo zigbuild` for Linux (musl static) and standard `cargo build --release` for macOS. For glibc compatibility on Linux, pin to `--target x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.2.17` via cargo-zigbuild.
3. **Homebrew formula:** simple bottle pointing at the GitHub release artifacts. Tap setup will live at `bjarneo/clitunes` or similar (final tap name set during release).
4. **AUR PKGBUILD:** builds from source via `cargo install --root pkgdir/usr/`.
5. **README structure:** project pitch, screenshots (from Units 17, 18, 19 — three-visualisers side-by-side and first-run), 3-line install instructions, link to docs.
6. **CHANGELOG:** starting with v1.0.0 entry summarising slices 1–5.

## Test scenarios

- Happy: tag `v1.0.0`, CI builds 4 artifacts, uploads them, `brew install clitunes` installs the macOS arm64 binary on a fresh macOS system, `clitunes` runs and shows the first-run picker.
- Edge: glibc version mismatch on Linux release. `cargo zigbuild` with `--target x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.2.17` to pin to a portable baseline.

## Verification

- A v1.0.0 release exists on GitHub with 4 artifacts and checksums.
- Manual install via Homebrew on a clean macOS box succeeds and runs.
- Manual install via `cargo install clitunes` on a clean Linux box succeeds and runs.

## v1 ships when this unit is verified

After Unit 20, clitunes v1.0.0 is shipped. The next plan (v1.1) covers Spotify integration via librespot, additional visualisers, and the variety test that was deferred from PF9.

title: Unit 11: SPMC cross-process PCM ring (Phase A spike + Phase B implementation/fallback)
description:
# Unit 11: SPMC cross-process PCM ring

**Phase:** 3 (Slice 3). **Parent epic:** clitunes-d50.

## Goal

Provide a cross-process delivery path for the PCM stream so multiple clients can independently consume the same audio for visualisation. Per round-1 D14, this MUST be SPMC (single producer, multiple consumers) — multiple `--pane visualiser` clients attached to the same daemon must each get their own consumer view, not fight over a single shared cursor.

**This is the highest-risk unit in the v1 plan.** The crates that look like they should solve the problem don't:
- `rtrb` is **SPSC only** — its `Consumer` cannot be cloned. Round-2 review F1 caught this; round-1 plan called it SPMC and was wrong.
- `ringbuf::SharedRb` is **in-process only** — it lives in process-local heap, not in shared memory. Cannot be mmap'd across processes.
- There is **no off-the-shelf Rust SPMC shared-memory ring crate.**

So Unit 11 is split into a 3-day spike (Phase A) followed by an implementation that branches based on the spike's go/no-go.

## Phase A: Spike (3 working days, hard budget)

**Subtask bead:** see clitunes-XXXX (Unit 11A spike).

Build a minimal SPMC shared-memory ring with `loom` model checking and a cross-process delivery test. Validate cross-process memory ordering on aarch64 Apple Silicon AND x86_64 Linux.

**Decision rule:** Phase A produces one of three outcomes:

1. **PASS** — `loom` accepts the ring under producer/2-consumer interleavings on `--release`, AND a cross-process test (1 producer process + 2 consumer processes communicating via the ring at 48kHz stereo for 60 seconds) shows zero data corruption AND zero overrun-detection failures. Phase B implements the real shm ring.

2. **FAIL — implementation path issue** (e.g., a fixable design bug). 1 day extension allowed; if still failing, treat as full FAIL.

3. **FAIL** — the spike cannot deliver a working SPMC shm ring within the budget. Phase B branches to the **fallback path: per-consumer mpsc fan-out + Unix datagram delivery** (see below).

The spike doc at `docs/spikes/2026-04-11-spmc-pcm-ring-spike.md` records the outcome with raw measurements and the architectural recommendation.

## Phase B: Implementation (branches on spike result)

**Subtask bead:** see clitunes-XXXX (Unit 11B implementation).

### Phase B path 1: Real shm ring (if Phase A passes)

- Backing store: `shm_open` + `mmap` on Linux (`/dev/shm`) and macOS (`$TMPDIR` or POSIX shm via `shm_open`). The shm region holds a header (sequence counters, metadata) + a power-of-2-sized PCM frame buffer.
- **Layout:** producer holds the only writable mapping. Consumers mmap as `PROT_READ` only (per round-2 SEC-008 — a writable consumer mapping lets one client corrupt the ring for all other clients).
- **Ordering:** producer uses `Ordering::Release` on the write-sequence counter; consumers use `Ordering::Acquire`. The ring tracks per-consumer cursors as separate atomic u64 values stored OUTSIDE the shm region (in the daemon's heap), so consumer crash doesn't corrupt producer state.
- **Overrun detection:** consumers check that the producer's write sequence hasn't advanced past their cursor by more than the buffer size between reads. On overrun, the consumer's `read()` returns `Err(Overrun { lost_frames })` and the consumer repositions to the producer's current cursor. **The visualiser is required to handle overrun by skipping a frame, NOT by silently rendering torn samples.**
- **Capacity:** 65_536 stereo frames (~680ms @ 48kHz). Tunable.

### Phase B path 2: mpsc fan-out + Unix datagram fallback (if Phase A fails)

- Daemon-side broadcast thread reads from the in-process PCM ring and pushes frames into a `Vec<UnixDatagramSender>`, one per connected client.
- Each client opens a Unix datagram socket (per-client path under `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/clitunes/pcm-<pid>.sock`).
- The daemon's broadcast thread `send_to`s each datagram socket with chunks of 256–1024 frames per packet.
- Slow consumers: per round-2 D14, on `try_send` failure (kernel send buffer full), the daemon DROPS the packet for that consumer and increments an `overruns` counter. **No back-pressure on the producer.**
- Trade-off: ~1.5× CPU vs the shm ring (memcpy + syscall per frame batch + datagram framing) but correctness collapses to "Tokio's mpsc + the kernel's UnixDatagram are correct" — no novel concurrency code, no loom.

The branch decision is documented in the spike doc and the implementation doc.

## Files

- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/pcm/spmc_ring.rs` (Phase A spike + Phase B path 1) — the shm ring implementation.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/pcm/datagram_fanout.rs` (Phase B path 2) — the fallback path.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/pcm/cross_process_api.rs` — the public API that abstracts whichever backend Phase B chose. Visualiser code never sees the shm-vs-datagram distinction.
- `docs/spikes/2026-04-11-spmc-pcm-ring-spike.md` — Phase A deliverable.
- `crates/clitunes-engine/tests/pcm_spmc_ring_loom.rs` — loom model check (Phase A).
- `crates/clitunes-engine/tests/pcm_cross_process_smoketest.rs` — 1-producer 2-consumer integration test (Phase A and Phase B).
- `crates/clitunes-engine/tests/pcm_overrun_handling_tests.rs` (Phase B).

## Test scenarios (Phase B, both paths)

- Happy: 1 producer + 2 consumers, 60 seconds at 48kHz stereo, both consumers see identical frame data (verify by hashing the consumed stream).
- Happy: 1 producer + 2 consumers running independent FFTs, both produce the same magnitude spectrum on a deterministic input source.
- Edge: consumer 1 stalls for 1 second (synthetic). Producer keeps writing. Consumer 1 detects overrun on resume, repositions, continues. Consumer 2 was unaffected.
- Edge: consumer 2 crashes (process killed). Producer notices via the consumer's cursor not advancing for >5s; daemon reaps the dead consumer's per-consumer state.
- Edge: producer is preempted for 50ms. Both consumers see normal data when producer resumes; no torn writes.
- Stress: 4 consumers running for 5 minutes. Memory stable. No overruns under normal load.
- Performance: per-frame end-to-end latency (producer write → consumer read) ≤2ms p99 on M1 + Linux.
- Security: consumer mmap is `PROT_READ` (per SEC-008). Verified by attempting a write from a consumer, expecting SIGSEGV.

## Verification

- Phase A spike doc exists and records the decision.
- Phase B implementation passes all integration tests.
- D8/D14 architectural commitments are honored: visualiser code is rendering-path-agnostic AND ring-backend-agnostic.

## Risks (per round-2 plan additions)

- **SPMC shm ring complexity** — Phase A may fail. Phase B fallback exists.
- **Cross-process memory ordering on aarch64** — Phase A's loom must cover this. Phase B fallback side-steps the problem entirely.

title: Cross-cutting: tracing/logging infrastructure + e2e test harness
description:
# Cross-cutting infrastructure: tracing + e2e harness

**Phase:** 1 (lands immediately after Unit 2 workspace scaffold; all later units depend on it). **Parent epic:** clitunes-ph0.

## Why this exists as its own bead

Polish round 1 of the bead graph identified that individual unit beads each list "test scenarios" but assume — without saying so — that there's a logging convention and e2e test harness in place to express those tests against. Without this bead, every implementer would invent their own ad-hoc logging and e2e patterns and the project would end up with a fractured testing surface. This bead establishes the convention ONCE so every subsequent unit can implement against it without re-inventing.

This is also where the polish prompt's mandate ("comprehensive unit tests AND e2e test scripts with great, detailed logging") becomes load-bearing infrastructure rather than per-bead boilerplate.

## Goal

Three deliverables:
1. **Tracing/logging convention** — `tracing` crate setup, structured fields, span hierarchy, EnvFilter wiring, rolling file appender. Both `clitunes` and `clitunesd` initialise the same way.
2. **E2E test harness** — `tests/e2e/` directory with a shell-driven test runner (`tests/e2e/run.sh`) that brings up the daemon, exercises a scenario, captures logs, asserts on outcomes, and tears down cleanly.
3. **CI integration** — the GitHub Actions matrix in Unit 2 gains an `e2e` job that runs the harness on every PR.

## Requirements traceability

Every unit's "test scenarios" section assumes this harness exists. Specifically supports the e2e bullets in Units 1, 7, 11, 16, plus the implicit e2e needs of Units 5/6/8/9/10/12/13/14/15/17/18/19/20.

## Files

- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/observability/mod.rs` — `init_tracing(component: &str, log_dir: &Path)` factory. Configures:
  - `tracing-subscriber` with `EnvFilter` (default `clitunes=info,clitunes_engine=info,warn`).
  - `tracing-appender::rolling::daily(log_dir, format!("{component}.log"))` for file output.
  - `fmt::layer().with_target(true).with_thread_ids(true).with_file(true).with_line_number(true)` for structured stderr output.
  - JSON format gated behind `--log-format=json` flag for machine consumption (used by e2e harness assertions).
- `crates/clitunes-engine/src/observability/spans.rs` — span name constants so every component agrees on the hierarchy:
  - `clitunesd::startup`, `clitunesd::idle_timer`, `clitunesd::client_session`
  - `audio::decoder`, `audio::ring::write`, `audio::ring::read`
  - `radio::stream`, `radio::icy_parse`, `radio::reconnect`
  - `tui::frame`, `tui::picker`, `tui::layout`
  - `viz::auralis::frame`, `viz::tideline::frame`, `viz::cascade::frame`
  - `control::request`, `control::response`
- `tests/e2e/run.sh` — bash driver. Subcommands:
  - `tests/e2e/run.sh all` — run every scenario.
  - `tests/e2e/run.sh <scenario>` — run one.
  - Each scenario lives in `tests/e2e/scenarios/<name>.sh` and gets a fresh `$CLITUNES_HOME` (tmpdir), fresh socket path, fresh log dir.
- `tests/e2e/scenarios/00-smoke-spawn-daemon.sh` — start daemon, send `ping`, expect `pong`, kill, verify clean exit.
- `tests/e2e/scenarios/01-picker-first-launch.sh` — fresh `$CLITUNES_HOME`, launch `clitunes`, expect picker, simulate selection (via test-only `--pick-station=<uuid>` flag), verify state.toml mode 0600.
- `tests/e2e/scenarios/02-radio-play-and-scrobble.sh` — start daemon, send `play <uuid>`, wait 5s, send `now-playing`, expect non-empty title, verify ICY metadata was parsed (assert tracing field `radio.icy_parsed=true` in the JSON log).
- `tests/e2e/scenarios/03-visualiser-frame-pacing.sh` — start daemon, attach a `--pane viz` client, run for 10s, parse JSON logs, assert `viz::auralis::frame` p99 latency below the committed bar from Unit 1.
- `tests/e2e/scenarios/04-graceful-shutdown.sh` — start daemon, send `quit`, expect socket file removed, expect process exit code 0, expect no orphan child processes.
- `tests/e2e/scenarios/05-idle-exit.sh` — start daemon with `--idle-timeout=5s`, do not connect, wait 6s, expect daemon process gone, socket removed.
- `tests/e2e/scenarios/06-crash-recovery.sh` — start daemon, kill -9, restart, expect new daemon, expect stale socket cleaned up (per Unit 9 stale-socket detection).
- `tests/e2e/lib/assertions.sh` — bash helpers: `assert_log_field`, `assert_file_mode`, `assert_process_alive`, `assert_process_dead`, `wait_for_log_line`.
- `tests/e2e/README.md` — how to run, how to add scenarios, how the JSON log assertions work.
- `.github/workflows/e2e.yml` — runs `tests/e2e/run.sh all` on Linux x86_64 + Linux aarch64 + macOS arm64. Uploads logs as artifacts on failure.

## The logging convention (what every implementer must follow)

- **Spans, not logs.** Anything that has a duration or a context gets a span. Anything that's a discrete event gets a `tracing::info!` / `warn!` / `error!`.
- **Structured fields, never string interpolation.** `info!(station_uuid=%uuid, "tuning")` not `info!("tuning to {uuid}")`. The e2e harness asserts on field values; string interpolation defeats this.
- **Error events at boundaries only.** Errors that propagate up should be logged exactly once, at the boundary where they're handled (typically the request handler or the main loop). Not at every layer they pass through.
- **No PII in logs.** Station UUIDs and titles are fine. User config paths are fine. Lyrics, full file paths to user music, and IP addresses of the user's machine are NOT fine. Per round-2 SEC-004 the sanitiser also runs on metadata before logging.
- **Performance budget.** Tracing overhead must be ≤2% of frame time at 60fps. The fmt layer is gated behind `EnvFilter` so disabled spans cost a single atomic load. JSON output is opt-in for tests only.

## E2E harness contract

Every scenario script:
1. Sets `CLITUNES_HOME=$(mktemp -d)` and `CLITUNES_LOG_FORMAT=json`.
2. Sources `tests/e2e/lib/assertions.sh`.
3. Starts the daemon (or relies on auto-spawn).
4. Exercises the scenario via `clitunes` subcommands or direct socket writes.
5. Asserts on log fields using `jq` against the JSON log file.
6. Tears down the daemon (`clitunes quit` or `kill`).
7. Removes `CLITUNES_HOME`.
8. Returns 0 on success, non-zero with a clear message on failure.

The harness MUST be deterministic. No flaky timing-dependent assertions; use `wait_for_log_line` with bounded polling, never bare `sleep`. Where timing is intrinsic (frame pacing test), the assertion runs over a 10-second sample with statistical bounds, not a single-frame check.

## Approach

1. Land `init_tracing()` and the span constants in `clitunes-engine/src/observability/`. Wire it from both `clitunes` and `clitunesd` main functions.
2. Build the bash harness skeleton (`run.sh`, `lib/assertions.sh`, README).
3. Land the 7 scenarios as stubs that exit 0 (so CI passes). Each subsequent unit fills in its scenario.
4. Wire the GitHub Actions `e2e.yml` workflow.
5. Document the logging convention in `docs/conventions/logging.md` so it's discoverable from outside the bead graph.

## Test scenarios (for the harness itself)

- The harness runner exits non-zero when ANY scenario fails. Verified by injecting a deliberately failing scenario in CI dry-run.
- A scenario that times out (e.g., `wait_for_log_line` exceeds 30s) returns non-zero with a "scenario timed out" message and uploads the partial log.
- The harness is idempotent: running `tests/e2e/run.sh all` twice in a row produces the same result with no leaked tmpdirs (verified by listing `/tmp` before/after).
- Running the harness without the daemon binary built fails fast with a clear "build clitunesd first" message, not a confusing socket error.
- JSON log assertions actually fail when the field value is wrong (verified by injecting a wrong value and confirming the assertion catches it).

## Verification

- `tests/e2e/run.sh all` exits 0 on a fresh checkout after `cargo build --release`.
- Every subsequent unit's PR adds at least one scenario script under `tests/e2e/scenarios/` OR explicitly explains in the PR description why an e2e test isn't applicable (e.g., pure-internal refactors).
- The `.github/workflows/e2e.yml` workflow runs on every PR and blocks merge on failure.
- `docs/conventions/logging.md` exists and is referenced from the workspace `README.md`.

## What this unit does NOT include

- Per-unit test scenarios — those live on each unit's bead.
- Performance regression detection (deferred; v1 ships without it).
- Synthetic load generation for stress tests (Unit 19 adds this for first-pixel measurement).

## How later units depend on this

Every unit's "test scenarios" section is implicitly upgraded: any bullet that says "happy:" or "edge:" or "stress:" can be implemented as either a `#[test]` (unit test using the tracing layer) OR as a `tests/e2e/scenarios/*.sh` script using the harness. The bead writer's intent is preserved — this bead just makes both options available.
close_reason: xb2 done: observability::init_tracing(component) with JSON/text toggle via CLITUNES_LOG_FORMAT=json. Named span constants. docs/conventions/logging.md convention doc. E2E harness: tests/e2e/run.sh, lib/assertions.sh, scenarios/01_slice1_calibration.sh. .github/workflows/{ci,e2e}.yml with D15 depgraph grep. Harness validated: 7/7 assertions pass on slice-1 binary.

title: Unit 1: wgpu→Kitty throughput spike (Phase 0)
description:
# Unit 1: wgpu→Kitty throughput spike

**Phase:** 0 (Slice 0). **Parent epic:** clitunes-xro.

## Goal

Build a working code sample that renders to a wgpu texture and streams it as f=24 (RGBA) Kitty graphics frames at the committed framerate for 60 seconds without exceeding the pre-committed p99 thresholds. Test on M1+Ghostty, M1+Kitty, Linux+Kitty, Linux+WezTerm. Produce a spike doc with raw measurements + go/no-go decision + architectural recommendation. **The entire visualiser-first identity of clitunes hangs on this spike.**

## Pre-committed numerical thresholds

**60fps bar:** `p99 ≤ 16ms` AND `p95 ≤ 14ms` per frame (render → readback → encode → write).
**30fps bar:** `p99 ≤ 33ms` AND `p95 ≤ 25ms`.

**Decision rule (mechanical, no judgment):**
- ≥2 of 4 platforms hit the 60fps bar → commit 60fps target for v1.
- Else ≥3 of 4 platforms hit the 30fps bar → commit 30fps target for v1.
- Else spike fails. v1 branches to a unicode-block CPU fallback at lower visual ceiling, and the project's identity claim is rewritten before any production code lands.

## Hard budget

5 working days. If 5 days pass without a clean go/no-go, the spike is force-decided as failed. No extensions.

## Files

- `crates/clitunes-spike/Cargo.toml` (separate spike crate, deleted after Phase 1; spike code is NOT directly imported into slice 1).
- `crates/clitunes-spike/src/main.rs` (driver: render N test frames, time each, print histogram).
- `crates/clitunes-spike/src/wgpu_pipeline.rs` (wgpu device init, render pass, texture readback via `Buffer::map_async` with the dedicated poll-thread pattern).
- `crates/clitunes-spike/src/kitty_writer.rs` (Kitty graphics protocol writer: APC start, base64 encode, transmission control, image placement).
- `crates/clitunes-spike/src/test_pattern.rs` (deterministic test pattern: rotating gradient + bouncing rectangle so frame-to-frame diff is non-zero).
- `docs/spikes/2026-04-11-wgpu-kitty-throughput-spike.md` (DELIVERABLE — measurements per platform, decision, architectural recommendation, fallback paths).

## Approach

1. **wgpu init:** request `Backends::PRIMARY`, prefer Metal on macOS / Vulkan on Linux. Print adapter name + driver version to spike doc. No `Surface` (windowless rendering — we render to an off-screen texture and read it back).
2. **Render target:** `wgpu::Texture` with `TextureFormat::Rgba8UnormSrgb`, dimensions matching the target Kitty cell-pixel area (start with 1920×1080, fall back to 1024×512 if readback exceeds budget).
3. **Render pass:** simple compute or fragment shader drawing the test pattern. Time the GPU work separately from the readback so the spike doc shows where the budget is spent.
4. **Readback (the load-bearing operation):** copy texture → mapped buffer → wait for `Buffer::map_async`. **Use the dedicated poll-thread pattern** — a background thread runs `device.poll(Maintain::Wait)` continuously so `map_async` completion is not blocked by the main thread's render cadence. Double-buffer the staging buffer (ping-pong) so the GPU can render frame N+1 while the CPU drains frame N's mapped buffer.
5. **Kitty encode:** base64-encode the RGBA bytes, chunk into 4096-byte payloads per the Kitty graphics protocol spec, emit `\x1b_Gf=24,s=W,v=H,m=1;<base64>\x1b\\` for each chunk with `m=0` on the final chunk. **Re-use the same image ID across frames** so the terminal updates in place rather than allocating a new image per frame.
6. **Temp file path** (if using `f=100` PNG mode as a fallback): per round-2 finding F2, the Kitty graphics protocol REQUIRES the temp file path to contain the literal substring `tty-graphics-protocol`. Use `mkstemp` template `tty-graphics-protocol-clitunes-XXXXXX.rgba` with `O_NOFOLLOW | O_EXCL` mode 0600. (This is also where SEC-003 lands — `tempfile::NamedTempFile` is not sufficient because it doesn't guarantee the right flags on all platforms.)
7. **Measurement loop:** render 1800 frames (60 seconds @ 30fps; 3600 @ 60fps). Time each frame end-to-end (render → readback → encode → write). Build a histogram. Print p50, p95, p99, p99.9, max.

## Test scenarios

- **Happy:** M1 Pro + Ghostty, 1920×1080, hits 60fps bar (p99 ≤ 16ms p95 ≤ 14ms) over 1800 frames.
- **Happy:** Linux x86_64 + Kitty, same.
- **Edge:** M1 + WezTerm — WezTerm Kitty graphics coverage is known to lag (per wezterm#2756, #6334). If it fails the 60fps bar but passes 30fps, that's still a pass per decision rule.
- **Edge:** Linux + WezTerm — same caveat.
- **Failure:** any platform fails to even produce visible output (Kitty protocol parse error, image position wrong, terminal hangs). Document the failure in the spike doc with the offending escape sequence dump and an evaluation of whether the failure is fixable in a day or whether it disqualifies the platform from v1.
- **Stress:** run the spike continuously for 5 minutes (not just 60 seconds). Verify there's no slow leak — RSS stable, no temp file accumulation, no growing latency.

## Verification

- `docs/spikes/2026-04-11-wgpu-kitty-throughput-spike.md` exists with:
  - Adapter info per platform.
  - Raw histogram (p50/p95/p99/p99.9/max) per platform.
  - Decision (60fps / 30fps / fail) per platform.
  - Aggregate decision per the decision rule.
  - Architectural recommendation: which staging strategy, which encoding (f=24 raw vs f=100 PNG), which terminals are committed for v1.
  - Fallback path commitments if the spike fails (drop to CPU unicode-block visualiser, rewrite identity).
- The spike code is committed to `crates/clitunes-spike/` and ships with a `cargo run -p clitunes-spike --release` reproducer.
- Slice 1 (Unit 4) imports the architectural pattern from this spike but reimplements against the real Visualiser trait. **The spike crate is deleted at the end of Phase 1.**

## Risks

- wgpu Metal readback >10ms p99 on M1 (high impact, medium likelihood). Fallbacks: shrink texture, JPEG encode, drop wgpu.
- No public Rust prior art for this exact pipeline (confirmed). Treat the spike as the prior art; document the working pattern.
- `/dev/shm` not available on macOS — for the spike use `$TMPDIR` on macOS and `/dev/shm` on Linux (documented in transport selection).
- Kitty graphics protocol coverage varies — WezTerm in particular may force a "Kitty + Ghostty + Rio only" v1 commitment if it can't be made to work.

## What this unit does NOT include

- The real `Visualiser` trait (Unit 4).
- The cross-process PCM ring (Unit 11).
- Auralis-specific shader code (Unit 4).
- Any production code that ships with v1. Spike code is throwaway.
close_reason: Phase 0 spike PASS on M1 Max (1024x512 p99=3.11ms, 1920x1080 p99=6.37ms). 60fps committed provisionally; cross-platform validation deferred to Unit 19. See docs/spikes/2026-04-11-wgpu-kitty-throughput-spike.md

title: Phase 0 — Slice 0: Pre-flight wgpu→Kitty throughput spike
description:
# Phase 0: Pre-flight wgpu→Kitty throughput spike

**Plan reference:** docs/plans/2026-04-11-001-feat-clitunes-v1-implementation-plan.md, Phase 0 (Slice 0).

## Why this phase exists

This phase is the load-bearing spike for the entire visualiser-first identity of clitunes. The plan stakes the project's identity on "Ghostty of TUI music apps" — flagship-quality terminal visualisers rendered through the Kitty graphics protocol from a wgpu backend. **Until we measure this end-to-end on real hardware, we don't know if it's feasible.** If the spike fails, all of slices 1-5 must branch to a CPU-rendered fallback at lower visual ceiling, which would change the project's identity claim.

## Pre-committed numerical thresholds

**60fps bar:** p99 ≤ 16ms AND p95 ≤ 14ms.
**30fps bar:** p99 ≤ 33ms AND p95 ≤ 25ms.

**Decision rule:**
- ≥2 of 4 platforms hit 60fps → commit 60fps target.
- Else ≥3 of 4 hit 30fps → commit 30fps target.
- Else spike fails → branch to unicode-block fallback.

These thresholds are pre-committed (set before measurement) to prevent post-hoc rationalisation of mediocre numbers.

## Success criteria

- A working code sample renders a wgpu texture and streams it as f=24 (RGBA) Kitty graphics frames at the committed framerate for 60 seconds without falling below p99.
- Tested on M1+Ghostty, M1+Kitty, Linux+Kitty, Linux+WezTerm.
- Spike doc committed to docs/spikes/ with raw measurements, decision, and architectural recommendation.

## Hard budget

5 days. If 5 days pass without a clean go/no-go, the team makes a forced architecture decision (most likely: drop wgpu, ship CPU-rendered Auralis at lower visual ceiling).

## Deliverable

Phase 0 produces ONE bead's worth of work (Unit 1) plus the spike doc. No production code from this phase enters slice 1 unchanged — slice 1 imports the architectural pattern but reimplements against the real `Visualiser` trait.
close_reason: Phase 0 epic complete: Unit 1 spike passed.

title: Phase 5 — Slice 5: First-run polish + distribution
description:
# Phase 5: First-run polish + distribution

**Plan reference:** Phase 5, Slice 5.

## What slice 5 ships

v1.0.0 tagged release. Single static binary per platform (M1 macOS, x86_64 macOS, x86_64 Linux glibc, aarch64 Linux glibc) installable via Homebrew, `cargo install clitunes`, or AUR. README with the 3-visualiser identity, screenshots from Units 17/18/19, install instructions, link to docs.

## Why this is the smallest possible polish phase

Slice 5 is **only** first-run polish + distribution. Everything else has shipped in slices 1-4. The temptation to slip "one more feature" into slice 5 is the main risk. Resist it.

## Units in this phase

- **Unit 19** — First-run UX polish + time-to-first-pixel measurement: `--measure-startup` instrumentation, run on 4 platforms, polish picker visual integration with Auralis behind it, commit `docs/SC1-validation.md` with measurements.
- **Unit 20** — Distribution: release CI matrix (4 platforms), Homebrew formula, AUR PKGBUILD, README rewrite, CHANGELOG, LICENSE finalisation, tag v1.0.0.

## Success gate

- v1.0.0 release exists on GitHub with 4 binary artifacts and checksums.
- `brew install clitunes` on a clean macOS box succeeds and runs.
- `cargo install clitunes` on a clean Linux box succeeds and runs.
- `docs/SC1-validation.md` shows ≤3000 ms first-pixel time on ≥3 platform/terminal combinations.
- README screenshots include the three-visualisers side-by-side.
